behaviors.description: Renamed files appear in a separate 'renamed' category, not counted as separate delete+add
behaviors.justification: Real failure: git rename detection is broken (wrong git flags, wrong status code parsing). Mocking git output won't catch bugs in git invocation. Requires actual git subprocess on a repo with a real rename operation to verify 'R' status codes are handled correctly.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real git repository, add a file, commit it, rename the file in a new commit, then run the tool. Verify the output shows exactly 1 renamed file (not 1 deleted + 1 added). Run `git diff HEAD~1 --name-status` to confirm git itself reports the rename.

behaviors.description: Binary files in the repository do not crash the tool; binary files are included in output counts
behaviors.justification: Real failure: the tool crashes or enters an infinite loop when encountering binary data. Mocking git's 'binary file' markers won't test how the code actually handles real binary artifacts (e.g., attempting text parsing, streaming large files). Requires actual binary files in a real repo and real git subprocess.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real git repository, add a mix of text and binary files (e.g., a PNG image, a PDF), make changes to the binary file (add, modify, or delete), run the tool, and verify: (1) the tool exits cleanly without crashing, and (2) the binary file is counted in the appropriate output category.

behaviors.description: A repository with zero commits produces an empty report, not an error
behaviors.justification: Real failure: the tool crashes or throws an error when git has no commit history. The code likely assumes `git log` returns output; on a zero-commit repo, git behaves differently. Must run actual git commands against a real empty repository to catch this edge case.
behaviors.test_description: Initialize a bare git repository with no commits (`git init`), run the tool on it, and verify the output is an empty/valid report (no exceptions, no stderr error messages). Contrast with a mocked scenario where you could return an empty list without triggering the real code path that handles git errors.

behaviors.description: Reported counts of added, deleted, renamed, and modified files match the actual change history
behaviors.justification: Real failure: counting bugs in complex repos (merges, multiple commits, mixed file types). Integration tests with mocks can verify parsing logic in isolation, but can't catch all edge cases in how git output is formatted and aggregated. Must run real git commands on repositories with known, verifiable histories (e.g., commit 3 files, then delete 1, then modify 2).
behaviors.test_description: Create git repositories with known, simple commit histories (e.g., one commit adding 3 files; second commit deleting 1 and modifying 2; a merge scenario). Run the tool and validate counts against ground truth derived from `git diff`, `git log`, and repository file structure. Test with at least one multi-commit history to catch off-by-one or aggregation errors.

behaviors.description: Output is identical regardless of whether changes are staged or unstaged
behaviors.justification: Real failure: the tool internally branches on staging state (e.g., checking `git diff --cached` in some paths, `git diff` in others), causing output to vary. Requires real git staging state to expose this divergence. Mocks can't test whether the tool is inadvertently mixing HEAD, index, and working tree queries.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real git repository with commits, then stage some file changes and leave others unstaged. Run the tool and capture output A. Unstage those changes and stage different ones (or create a fresh unstaged set). Run the tool again and capture output B. Verify A and B are identical (or verify per spec that tool output is consistent—does not depend on staging state). If tool reports all changes, verify the same set is reported both times.
